query: Historical nonfarm payrolls surprise distribution - how much do initial releases deviate from consensus forecasts
answer:
Now let me compile and cross-validate the findings from all three research agents. One agent was able to pull actual FRED data showing the most recent payrolls levels and revisions, which is extremely valuable. Let me synthesize everything into a comprehensive report.

---

# Historical Nonfarm Payrolls Surprise Distribution: How Initial Releases Deviate from Consensus Forecasts

## 1. Overview

The "NFP surprise" — the difference between the Bureau of Labor Statistics' initial nonfarm payrolls release and the median economist consensus forecast (typically compiled by Bloomberg/Reuters from 60–80 economists) — is one of the most closely watched economic data points in financial markets. This report synthesizes historical statistics, recent data, and revision patterns.

## 2. Surprise Distribution Statistics

### Pre-COVID Era (2010–2019, ~120 months)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Mean surprise (Actual − Consensus) | +5K to +10K |
| Median surprise | ~+5K |
| Standard deviation | ~60K–75K |
| Interquartile range | −30K to +50K |
| 90th percentile range | −90K to +120K |
| Largest positive surprise | ~+200K |
| Largest negative surprise | ~−150K |

### Post-COVID Era (2021–2024)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Mean surprise | +40K to +60K |
| Standard deviation | ~90K–120K |
| Systematic pattern | Persistent under-prediction by consensus |

### Full Sample (2000–2024, excluding COVID months Mar–Jun 2020)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Mean surprise | +10K to +15K |
| Median surprise | ~+8K |
| Standard deviation | ~70K–85K |
| Mean absolute forecast error | ~65K–75K |
| Median absolute forecast error | ~47K |
| Distribution shape | Roughly normal, positive skew, fat tails (leptokurtic) |
| Months with surprise > 100K | ~15–20% |
| Surprises > 2σ frequency | ~8–10% (vs. 5% for normal distribution) |

## 3. Recent Monthly Surprise Data

### 2024 (Initial Releases vs. Consensus)

| Month | Actual (K) | Consensus (K) | Surprise (K) |
|-------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| Jan 2024 | +353 | +185 | **+168** |
| Feb 2024 | +275 | +198 | +77 |
| Mar 2024 | +303 | +200 | +103 |
| Apr 2024 | +175 | +243 | −68 |
| May 2024 | +272 | +185 | +87 |
| Jun 2024 | +206 | +191 | +15 |
| Jul 2024 | +114 | +176 | −62 |
| Aug 2024 | +142 | +164 | −22 |
| Sep 2024 | +254 | +150 | **+104** |
| Oct 2024 | +12 | +113 | **−101** |
| Nov 2024 | +227 | +200 | +27 |
| Dec 2024 | +256 | +160 | +96 |

**2024 Summary:** Mean surprise = +35K; Median = +51K; Std dev = ~85K; 8 of 12 months were positive surprises.

### 2025–2026 (From FRED Vintage Data)

| Month | Initial Release (K) | Consensus (K) | Notes |
|-------|-------------------|---------------|-------|
| Jan 2025 | +143 | ~+170 | −27K miss |
| Feb 2025 | +151 | — | Revised dramatically to +42K |
| Mar 2025 | +228 | — | Revised to +67K |
| Jan 2026 | +130 | +70 | +60K beat |
| Feb 2026 | −92 | — | Large negative surprise |
| Mar 2026 | +178 | — | Released Apr 3, 2026 |

## 4. Systematic Bias: Surprises Skew Positive in Expansions

There is strong evidence that NFP surprises are **regime-dependent**:

| Economic Regime | Mean Surprise | Notes |
|----------------|--------------|-------|
| Steady expansion | +5K to +15K | Mild positive bias |
| Early recovery | +30K to +80K | Strong positive bias |
| Late cycle/slowdown | −10K to +5K | Roughly unbiased |
| Recession | −50K to −100K | Negative bias |

Over multi-year samples, NFP beats consensus roughly **55–60%** of the time.

## 5. Consensus Forecast Dispersion

The spread among individual economist forecasts provides context on uncertainty:

| Period | IQR of Forecasts | Full Range | Std Dev of Forecasts |
|--------|-----------------|------------|---------------------|
| 2015–2019 | 40K–60K | 120K–180K | 25K–40K |
| 2021–2024 | 60K–100K | 200K–350K | 40K–70K |

Wider forecast dispersion correlates with larger absolute surprises.

## 6. Revision Patterns: Initial Releases Are Noisy

### Standard Monthly Revisions

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Mean first revision (1 month later) | +10K to +15K |
| Mean cumulative revision (2 months later) | +20K to +30K |
| Std dev of first revision | ~30K–40K |
| Std dev of cumulative revision | ~45K–55K |
| Share of first revisions that are positive | ~55–60% |

### Annual Benchmark Revisions (Applied Each February)

The BLS incorporates comprehensive QCEW data, producing often-massive benchmark revisions:

| Year | Benchmark Revision (K) |
|------|----------------------|
| 2024 (ref Mar 2024) | **−818K** |
| 2023 (ref Mar 2023) | −187K |
| 2022 (ref Mar 2022) | −306K |
| 2021 (ref Mar 2021) | +462K |
| 2019 (ref Mar 2019) | −514K |
| 2009 (ref Mar 2009) | −617K |
| **Mean absolute benchmark revision (2004–2024)** | **266K** |

### The 2025–2026 Benchmark Revision Was Historically Extreme

FRED vintage data reveals that the February 2026 benchmark revision revised 2025 employment levels **downward by approximately 1 million jobs** on average across all 2025 months:

- Initial reports for 2025 showed average monthly gains of ~+103K
- Revised data shows average monthly gains of only ~+10K
- Individual monthly revisions ranged from −9K to **−167K** (June 2025)
- 12 of 13 months examined were revised downward

## 7. Academic Literature

Key studies on NFP data quality and forecast accuracy:

- **Aruoba (2008)** – "Data Revisions Are Not Well Behaved" (Philadelphia Fed): Initial BLS releases are noisy and systematically revised upward during expansions.
- **Gilbert et al. (2017)** – Nonfarm payrolls initial releases revised ~+30K on average over two subsequent revisions, with std dev of ~45K–55K.
- **Swanson & van Dijk (2006)** – Found slight downward bias in initial releases (revisions tend to be positive), questioning rationality of initial estimates.
- **NY Fed/Philadelphia Fed research** – NFP surprise is the single most important driver of intraday Treasury, equity, and FX price moves on release day.

## 8. Key Takeaways for Modeling

1. **Standard deviation of ~70–85K** is the appropriate baseline for the surprise distribution in normal times; use ~90–120K in high-uncertainty periods.
2. **Positive bias of ~+10–15K** exists on average (actual tends to beat consensus in expansions).
3. **Fat tails**: Extreme surprises (>2σ) occur ~8–10% of the time vs. 5% expected under normality.
4. **Revisions are large**: Cumulative revisions average +25K with std dev ~48K, and annual benchmark revisions can reach hundreds of thousands.
5. **The initial release is a poor measure of "truth"**: The 2025 experience (initially reported ~+103K/month, revised to ~+10K/month) shows initial data can be dramatically misleading.
6. **Regime matters**: The surprise distribution shifts substantially across economic regimes.

---

sources.title: Bureau of Labor Statistics - Employment Situation Summary
sources.snippet: Primary source for monthly nonfarm payrolls initial releases. Historical releases used to compute surprise distributions.

sources.title: FRED - All Employees, Total Nonfarm (PAYEMS)
sources.snippet: FRED vintage data showing PAYEMS levels through March 2026 (last updated 2026-04-03). Used to compute monthly change revisions and benchmark impacts.

sources.title: ALFRED - Archival FRED Real-Time Data for PAYEMS
sources.snippet: Historical vintage data for all past releases of PAYEMS, enabling computation of revision statistics across initial and subsequent releases.

sources.title: BLS Current Employment Statistics - Benchmark Revisions
sources.snippet: Annual benchmark revision data showing cumulative adjustments to nonfarm payrolls. 2024 revision was -818K; 2025/2026 revision revised 2025 levels down ~1 million.

sources.title: Philadelphia Fed Real-Time Data Research Center
sources.snippet: Research on data revisions including Aruoba (2008) finding that initial BLS employment releases are noisy and systematically revised.

sources.title: Trading Economics - United States Non Farm Payrolls
sources.snippet: Historical NFP actual vs forecast data. Cross-validated 2024 monthly surprise figures.

sources.title: Bloomberg Economist Survey - NFP Consensus Forecasts
sources.snippet: Bloomberg compiles 60-80 economist forecasts for monthly NFP consensus. Dispersion data (IQR, range) derived from survey distributions.

key_facts: The standard deviation of NFP surprises (actual minus consensus) is approximately 70K-85K in normal times, widening to 90K-120K in high-uncertainty periods like 2021-2024.
NFP surprises exhibit a positive bias in expansions: the mean surprise is +10K to +15K over full samples (2000-2024 ex-COVID), rising to +40K-60K during 2021-2023.
NFP beats consensus roughly 55-60% of the time across multi-year samples.
The median absolute forecast error is approximately 47K, while the mean absolute error is 65K-75K.
Surprises exceeding 100K occur in roughly 15-20% of months.
The distribution has fat tails (leptokurtic): extreme surprises (>2 standard deviations) occur 8-10% of the time vs 5% expected under normality.
In 2024, the mean surprise was +35K with 8 of 12 months beating consensus; the largest positive surprise was January (+168K) and largest negative was October (-101K, hurricane/strike-distorted).
Monthly revisions are substantial: the mean first revision is +10K-15K, and cumulative two-month revision averages +20K-30K with std dev of 45K-55K.
Annual benchmark revisions (applied each February using QCEW data) averaged 266K in absolute terms from 2004-2024.
The 2026 benchmark revision revised 2025 employment levels down by ~1 million jobs, changing average monthly gains from ~+103K initially reported to ~+10K.
The 2024 benchmark revision was -818K (preliminary), the largest downward revision since 2009's -617K (later exceeded by the 2025 revision).
Consensus forecast dispersion (IQR of individual forecasts) ranges from 40K-60K in calm periods to 60K-100K in uncertain periods.
NFP surprise bias is regime-dependent: +5K to +15K in steady expansions, +30K to +80K in early recovery, roughly zero in late cycle, and -50K to -100K in recessions.
The Bloomberg/Reuters consensus typically includes 60-80 individual economist forecasts.
FRED vintage data shows 2025 monthly changes were revised downward in 12 of 13 months examined, with individual revisions ranging from -9K to -167K.
